issues.current_text: a new array containing the first `n` elements of this array, or all elements if `n` exceeds the array length
issues.problem: Does not mention the negative-`n` case. The implementation delegates to `slice(0, n)` which returns an empty array when `n` is negative or zero.
issues.suggestion: a new array containing the first `n` elements of this array, all elements if `n` exceeds the array length, or an empty array if `n` is non-positive

issues.current_text: a new array containing all elements of this array except the first `n`, or empty if `n` exceeds the array length
issues.problem: Does not mention behavior when `n` is negative or zero (a full copy is returned).
issues.suggestion: a new array containing all elements of this array except the first `n`, an empty array if `n` exceeds the array length, or a copy of all elements if `n` is non-positive

issues.current_text: a new array containing the elements of this array with `replaced` elements starting at `from` replaced by the elements of `other`
issues.problem: Main description notes edge cases (negative `from` treated as 0, `from` past the end appends, excess `replaced` is ignored). The @return could briefly acknowledge these, though they are documented above.
issues.suggestion: a new array consisting of the elements of this array with `replaced` elements starting at `from` replaced by the elements of `other` (subject to the edge cases noted above)

issues.current_text: a new array containing all elements of this array, with the specified element type `B`
issues.problem: Minor wording: phrasing 'with the specified element type `B`' is slightly redundant with the @tparam description and reads awkwardly.
issues.suggestion: a new `Array[B]` containing all elements of this array

general_feedback: The newly filled-in @return and @param descriptions are accurate, consistent in style (lowercase start, backticked code references), and align with the surrounding Scaladoc conventions. The `copyToArray` overloads use a consistent return description, and the append/prepend family is uniform. A few entries could be tightened by mentioning edge cases (negative arguments to take/drop, patch boundary behavior), but none of the additions are incorrect or misleading.
